data.title: Abercrombie & Fitch: Buy The Dip After Guidance Boost
data.description:
With the stock market looking increasingly jittery near all-time highs, my core strategy for managing my portfolio remains to reallocate the majority of my portfolio out of the expensive, large-cap tech plays that have dominated this year's gains while favoring more beaten-down small- and mid-cap stocks in other sectors that have their own growth catalysts to outperform the S&P 500.

Abercrombie & Fitch (NYSE:ANF), in my view, is a stock that is getting increasingly more attractive as it falls. The apparel company has lost ~50% of its value this year, despite a recent guidance increase that puts the stock at a very cheap P/E multiple.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Abercrombie in June, when the stock was trading in the low $80s. Though this is a position that has required a frustrating amount of patience, I think the company's guidance boost, its preservation of margins amid the tariff environment, and continued growth of the Hollister brand are key drivers to push Abercrombie stock higher, not to mention an ultra-low valuation. Amid undue and unjustified pessimism for this stock, I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating here.

Here is my updated long-term bull case for Abercrombie & Fitch:

  • Rebrand to basics appears to be driving positive comp sales growth for the company. Abercrombie has ditched its historic risqué image in favor of a more basic approach to young people's clothing, especially in jeans. This has yielded a successful return to positive comp sales for the company.
  • Hollister growth opportunity. The company's Hollister brand now generates more revenue than the eponymous Abercrombie brand and is still growing at a ~20% y/y clip. The success of Hollister may be due to its accessible price point, which is resonating with consumers in a budget-strapped environment.
  • A large buyback plan indicates management's confidence in undervaluation. As a clear signal that management believes the stock to be trading at a great price, Abercrombie & Fitch has an open, multi-year $1.3 billion share buyback program (roughly worth one-third of the company's current market cap), of which $1.1 billion remains unused. It plans to execute around ~$400 million in buybacks this year, which is amply supported by the ~$600 million of cash that the company has on its balance sheet (which is now unencumbered of debt).
  • Controlled tariff impact. The company has cited a 100 bps impact from tariffs on its imports from China and other markets, which is more than easily absorbed by the company's healthy gross margin profile.

Where we want to focus attention, however, is on the fact that Abercrombie actually boosted its full-year outlook, despite the downward motion in the stock over the past few months. Alongside Abercrombie's Q2 results, the company boosted its pro forma EPS expectations by 3% to $10.00-$10.50 (though this is still slightly below the original $10.40-$11.40 range that the company had before it cut its guidance in Q1). Abercrombie is now also expecting 5%-7% sales growth, versus 3%-6% previously.

At current share prices near $70, Abercrombie sits at a valuation of just 6.8x FY 2025 P/E (or just 5.6x FY 2025 P/E on an ex-cash basis, after considering the $603 million of cash on the company's latest balance sheet). This is an unbeatable valuation in today's market and a great way to diversify your portfolio away from expensive tech stocks.

Q2 download

Of course, there are some risks inherent in Abercrombie's financials, but that said, the stock's ultra-low valuation compensates for it. Let's now go through the company's latest Q2 results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Abercrombie's revenue grew 7% y/y to $2.31 billion, beating Wall Street's expectations of $2.30 billion (+6% y/y) by a one-point margin. Of course, the metric we're primarily concerned with is comparable sales, which, as a reminder, measures the revenue growth from existing store locations without including the impact of newly opened or newly closed stores. We note that Abercrombie's overall comp sales decelerated by one point to 3% y/y. This was driven by the Abercrombie brand's decline to -11% y/y, while Hollister still grew at a robust pace but decelerated four points y/y to 19% y/y, relative to 23% growth in Q1.

Cutting up the results by geography showcases an important story as well: we note that while the Americas have held up strong at 5% comps growth (accelerating one point relative to 4% growth in Q1), Europe decayed to a -5% y/y decline, representing 11 points of sequential deceleration for the company's second-largest market, which is just over 15% of revenue. Loss of customers in Europe is one of the core risks that Abercrombie faces, especially since Europe was driving double-digit growth for the company in FY 2024.

Speaking to the results by market on the Q2 earnings call, CEO Fran Horowitz noted as follows:

Regionally, the Americas achieved its 12th consecutive quarter of growth with net sales up 8% on continued traffic strength across direct channels. In EMEA, continued cross-channel growth in the U.K. was outweighed by softness in Germany and the remainder of European markets, with regional net sales lower by 1% against 16% growth in the second quarter of 2024. APAC continued to perform well, growing 12%, a nice cross-channel demand, while comparable sales grew 1% [...]

Looking to the second half of the year, we are increasing our full year 2025 net sales growth expectations based on our year-to-date results, supported by strong brand positioning, clean inventory, cross-channel traffic growth and our balance sheet.

We do note that Abercrombie is facing a tough retail environment in which many retail/apparel peers, such as Lululemon (LULU) and Gap (GAP), have reported much weaker or even negative comps, so relatively speaking, Abercrombie is holding up quite well, despite the sharp contraction in its P/E multiples.

On the profitability front, the company managed to offset a 3-point y/y decline in gross margins (driven by tariff costs, partially offset by favorable freight costs) with a 2-point efficiency in general and administrative costs. Operating income grew slightly y/y, while margins shed -80bps.

Key takeaways

Abercrombie & Fitch certainly isn't a squeaky-clean stock. We're mindful of continued contraction in the company's core Abercrombie brand, while sales trends in EMEA are also worrying. At the same time, however, Abercrombie is offsetting these headwinds successfully in building out the Hollister brand, while new top-tier partnerships, such as with the NFL, are boosting results in the Americas. Trading at such a low multiple of earnings for a company with zero debt is difficult to find in today's market. "Buy" here with confidence.

data.title: Patient Capital Management Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Are We There Yet?

Samantha McLemore

Short answer: Not yet—but we’re getting closer.Since March 2009, the S&P 500 has been a 10-bagger (~17% CAGR). Valuations are higher and speculation is louder, but institutions remain under-positioned, caution is pervasive, and the AI story is still unfolding—powerfully, but unevenly.Key Takeaways:

  • The real risk is over-caution. The past decade made this plain: HFRI ~4.3% vs. S&P 500 13.5% (2010 to 2019); endowments 6.8% vs. market 13.0% (2014 to 2024). Constant protection against drawdowns often sacrificed compounding.
  • Later innings ≠ last inning. We’re deep into a secular bull market that began at 12× forward earnings and now sits near ~23×. Sentiment remains skeptical (light institutional positioning; caution-heavy headlines), which is not typical of final peaks.
  • AI is transformative—and messy. Headlines warn of bubbles and capex risk; full interviews reveal nuanced optimism (Solomon, Bezos, Huang). Expect winners and losers, not a single narrative.
  • Category 1 vs. Category 2 errors. Investors obsess over being wrong (Type I) and underestimate the cost of inaction (Type II). Over time, missing big winners has been far costlier than enduring volatility.
  • Our stance. Patient, opportunistic, focused on avoiding permanent impairment, not volatility. We’ve owned what compounds (e.g., NVIDIA) and avoided capital-hungry “neo-clouds” where cash burn and valuation do not yet reconcile.

Why read on: to see how we square rising valuations with persistent skepticism, what we’re owning (and avoiding) in AI, and how we’re positioning for the end of the ride—whenever it arrives—without surrendering the power of compounding today.

Are We There Yet?

As a child, we rarely took vacations. Too expensive. A few times we drove 16 hours from Vermont to our grandparents’ house in South Carolina, a long and painful drive. We must have asked our parents a million times, “Are we there yet?” No wonder dad threatened to “cut a switch1.” Now I’m on the parent side of that classic question. Fortunately, air travel and Apple iPads have cut down the frequency!

The secular bull market that began in March 2009 has been characterized by a nearly constant fear that the end is nigh. “Are we there yet?”

The secular bull market gains have been stunning. In Peter Lynch parlance, from the 2009 lows to today, the S&P 500 produced a 10-bagger, compounding at 17.0% per year. It started with pure panic and depressed valuations. The S&P 500 traded at 12x forward 12-month earnings. Now, there’s significantly more optimism and valuations are almost double (23x forward 12-month earnings).

Unfortunately, most investors have failed to fully participate in the gains. Financial crisis trauma led to a costly, myopic focus on risk protection. Hedge funds, as measured by the HFRI Index, gained 4.3% per year on average in the 2010s, woefully lagging the S&P 500’s 13.5% annual return. A stark contrast to the outperformance they notched prior to the financial crisis.

To be sure, there are more signs of speculation now. AI deals announced for exorbitant sums with structures harkening back to disastrous Tech Bubble “vendor financing”. Young retail investors making bank on crypto and AI….YOLO.2 I even saw a recent X post claiming everyone in a room was trading on their brokerage sites.

Patient Capital has an annual employee stock picking contest where we pick 10 stocks for the year. Our non-investment team’s portfolios are up 41% on average for the year – nearly triple the S&P 500’s return. Every single administrative professional is beating the market. It’s not usually so easy to crush the market. We are in a bull market indeed.

Does that mean we’re “there”?

Institutional risk tolerance continues to be muted. University endowment returns have substantially lagged the market. Through the end of 2024, the NACUBO Commonfund Study of Endowments reported that endowments returned 6.8% per year over the preceding decade, a little over half the S&P 500’s 13.0% annual return.

Goldman Sachs (GS) recently reported light institutional fund positioning. Its sentiment indicator clocked its 30th consecutive negative reading, the third longest streak since 2015 (the two longer ones were both 32 weeks). Concerns, from tariffs and government shutdowns to AI capital spend and geopolitics, keep caution elevated.

At the Italian Tech Week in early October, Goldman Sachs CEO David Solomon said, “I see complacency around risk-taking… I wouldn’t be surprised if in the next 12 to 24 months we see a drawdown.” He predicted there would be winners and losers.

At the same conference, Jeff Bezos called the AI boom, “an industrial bubble.” He said markets struggle to differentiate between winners and losers during excitement, citing the unusual circumstance of 6-person startups being funded at $20B valuations.

Those cautionary headlines spread rapidly.

If you listen to the entire interviews, they sounded far more positive than suggested. Solomon said he wants to spend much more on AI but is constrained by earnings considerations.

Bezos said it’s hard to fathom how transformative AI is. He said the horizontal enabling layer will improve quality and productivity for literally every company. He said it’s the most exciting time to be alive because of three concurrent Golden Ages: space travel, AI and robotics.

Jensen Huang, CEO of Nvidia (NVDA), recently spoke on the B-squared podcast with Brad Gerstner. Jensen said he’s not concerned about an overbuild of AI in the near-term. “Until we fully convert all general purpose computing to accelerated computing and AI…I think the chances [of a glut] are low.”

OpenAI (OPENAI) and AMD (AMD) announced another large deal, spurred by what OpenAI said is a shortage of compute.

We’ve compared this period to the late 90s or Nifty Fifty periods, both of which entailed spectacular gains and ended with secular bull market peaks. However, many people got defensive way too early and missed out. Greenspan’s famous “irrational exuberance” line in late 1996 was followed by 116% market gains! Even after the crash, the market didn’t fall to the levels where he uttered that line.

Paul Tudor Jones, one of the greatest traders of our times, recently told CNBC, “It’s like 1999...the ingredients are in place for some kind of blow off” top…“If anything, now is so much more potentially explosive than 1999. You need to position yourself like it's October 1999. The Nasdaq doubled from October 1999 to March 2000.”

Everyone seems to agree we are in a bubble. However, the magnitude, scope, and even existence of a bubble is only ever clear in hindsight. A few things seem clear at present:

  1. AI is transformative with broad usefulness and impact. It’s attracting significant attention and capital.
  2. It’s costly to build AI infrastructure, increasing the risk of subpar returns.
  3. Judging from valuations and sentiment, we are in the later stages of the secular bull market. Whether that occurs in months or many years, no one knows.
  4. Speculative activity is growing. Bezos cited startup funding. OpenAI’s recent $500B valuation is 25x its 2021 GPT-3 post releases $20B valuation. OpenAI is reported to have committed to orders for over $1T in infrastructure spend.
  5. Significant skepticism still exists. Cautionary headlines fill the news. Nvidia, arguably the main economic beneficiary of the boom, trades for 33x next 12 months earnings, a far cry from Cisco’s peak above 100x. Markets are complex, adaptive systems, and people have learned from past bubbles.

So where does that leave us? Howard Marks’ Jan 2025 piece, “On Bubble Watch,” brilliantly explained bubbles and summed up the pros and cons of the current environment well. The key point, “For me, it’s psychological extremeness that marks a bubble.”

Bubbles don’t typically peak with the world on high alert. At peaks, even the most bearish have usually thrown in the towel on their skepticism. But who knows? We approach markets with a huge dose of humility. There’s far more we don’t know than we know. Vigilance and agility are paramount.

Over the past decade and a half, people have cost themselves much more by being too cautious. People are much more sensitive to Category 1 investing errors (acting when you shouldn’t), than Category 2 ones (failing to act). See ChatGPT’s Summary below. Given losses are twice as painful as gains are pleasurable, investors aren’t as sensitive to missing money-making opportunities as they should be.

Those endowments that earned 6.8% per year over the past decade turned $10,000 into $19,307. If they’d invested in an S&P index fund instead, their 13.0% (or close to it with minor fees) returns would have resulted in $33,946 instead, 1.8x as much.

The Tech Bubble crash was one of the worst in history, entailing a 49% peak-to-trough decline. It was a secular market peak that coincided with a bubble.

If a comparable crash occurred today, the ~$34K index fund balance would fall to $17,312. Endowments should fare better. If their market capture rate is proportional (52.3% of the market’s upside and downside), their losses would be 25.6%, yielding a final balance of $14,358.

The index fund would still produce a balance 20% higher, despite being exposed to bigger losses. Markets rise over time, and compounding is powerful. You’re financially better off capturing the volatility on both sides. Psychologically, though, you’re exposed.

Market crashes like the Tech Bubble have happened only 5 times in the past century, a rate of roughly once every 20 years. The former analysis actually understates the benefits of downside volatility tolerance. Using a compounding period of 20 years before a crash, the index investor winds up with more than twice as much as the endowment investor (detailed in table).

We did suffer two whopper ~50% crashes within a decade in the 2000s. First, the Tech Bubble, then the Financial Crisis. This experience colored a generation of investors. It’s an important origin of the heightened risk awareness we see today.

My longtime colleague, Bill Miller, often pointed out that when people think of risk they only think of the risk of loss if things go wrong, but risk is two-sided and asymmetrical. You can only lose 100% of what you have at risk, but if things go right, you have the possibility of making many hundreds or even thousands of times what you are risking.

People only tend to think about this risk of missing out on gains during the later stages of a bull market. Does that mean people should get more aggressive today? Not at all.

A cardinal sin in investing is doing today what you should have done many years ago. Unfortunately, this happens all the time. It’s easy to see what you should have done differently. The incentive to do something to fix performance problems is significant.

With valuations near historical highs and pockets of euphoria, more caution is warranted. Long-term return prospects are lower than the past decade given the starting point.

Another great line from The Money Game states, “The only real protection against all the vagaries…is to have an identity so firm it is not influenced by all the brouhaha in the marketplace.” It goes on, “The end object of investment is serenity, and serenity can only be achieved by the avoidance of anxiety, and to avoid anxiety you have to know who you are and what you’re doing.”

As for us, we aim to deliver strong, market-beating returns. We are patient, opportunistic, and behaviorally aware. We seek to prevent permanent impairments of capital, not volatility.

Impairment means you don’t make back losses. This usually results from deteriorating business fundamentals or overpaying for an asset. We seek to mitigate both risks. Further, we seek to mitigate the risk we underperform our benchmark.

To deliver on our objective of high, market beating returns, we will need to protect against impairments associated with secular bull market tops. Fortunately, value investing has proved protective after similar past peaks. Our identity is long-term, value investors. We compare market expectations to business fundamentals and intrinsic values. We seek to capitalize on disconnections between the two when we have a variant perception.

We believe AI is transformative. Not all transformations lead to excellent investment opportunities. Air travel changed the world, but airlines have been the poster children of terrible investments (we think some airlines are attractive currently).

We invested in Nvidia early last year because we believed the market miscategorized Nvidia as a hardware company with imminent earnings risk. We thought Nvidia’s software, networking and innovation cycle created a broader competitive advantage, and the opportunity was huge.

We still believe Nvidia is earning the bulk of AI economic returns (so far). It’s a fantastic company with great returns and growth at the center of an economic boom. In our view, it’s not expensive at 29x FY 2027 (calendar 2026) earnings, which are expected to grow at 40%+. Their products are still in high demand with shortages.

The enduring fear has been that demand will roll over at some point, creating an air pocket in earnings and the stock. It’s possible. We aren’t smart enough to predict when that might happen. But we aren’t there yet. Market expectations are clouded by that fear, while business fundamentals remain stellar – a disconnect we are happy to continue to exploit.

On the other hand, we’ve avoided the neoclouds. Demand growth has been astounding, but they are burning significant cash given the elevated capital intensity. JP Morgan (JPM) recently initiated on CoreWeave, the leader, with an overweight.

It projected 2029 free cash flow of $3-4B. With a $79B enterprise value, one needs to believe in significantly brighter prospects over some time horizon to justify the current price.

As Peter Lynch said during the height of the Tech Bubble, “That’s not to say there’s no such thing as an overvalued market, but there’s no point in worrying about it.”

“Mommy, are we there yet?” No dear, not yet. But we’re getting closer.

Stay tuned for more about our positioning and individual holdings in our Q3 portfolio recap.

data.title: Enerpac Tool Group's Surge Isn't Proof You Should Pile In
data.description:
October 16th was a fantastic day for shareholders of Enerpac Tool Group Corp. (NYSE:EPAC). Shares of the company shot up 6% after management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. Top line and bottom line results came in above what analysts were hoping to see, and management came out with robust guidance for 2026. This paints the picture of a business that is continually growing and improving. In the long run, I have no doubt that this enterprise will do very well operationally.

But you should not confuse operational performance with share price performance. The fact of the matter is that the company continues to be priced at very high multiples. And that has been reflected in its returns as of late.

Back in June of this year, I decided to reaffirm the company as a Hold candidate. This followed a painful dip that the stock had experienced. My thought in approaching the business at that time was that perhaps it represented a buying opportunity. But sure enough, I decided that the stock was too expensive for my liking.

Since then, even with this 6% jump that we saw in midday trading on October 16th, the stock is up only 2.3% compared to the 8.1% rise that the broader market has enjoyed. And even though I feel optimistic about the long term outlook for the company, I cannot in good faith upgrade EPAC stock at this time.

A great business at a not-so-great price

After the market closed on October 15th, management came out with financial results for the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year. During that time, Enerpac Tool Group generated revenue of $167.5 million. That's an increase of 5.5% over the $158.7 million the company reported the previous year. This jump came in spite of the fact that the company saw a 1.8% drop in organic revenue.

The real driver was its acquisition of DTA The Smart Move, S.A., a player in the industrial heavy loads transportation industry that focuses on the production and sale of mobile robotic solutions. That company was ultimately purchased in September of last year in exchange for $26.7 million, with the potential for another €12 million in earnouts over the span of three years.

The revenue reported by management ended up coming in $2.8 million above what analysts were hoping to see. But that's not the only win that shareholders enjoyed. Earnings per share grew year over year from $0.43 to $0.52, taking net profits up from $23.4 million to $28.1 million. The earnings per share reported by the company ended up being $0.02 greater than what analysts were hoping to see. Adjusted earnings per share, meanwhile, increased from $0.50 to $0.52, taking overall adjusted net profits up from $27.5 million to $27.9 million. Analysts were expecting adjusted earnings per share of only $0.51 for the quarter, so the company beat expectations there as well. The chart above shows that cash flow metrics for the business improved also.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for 2025 compared to the same time in 2024. Revenue, profits, adjusted profits, and cash flows all got better year-over-year. This is exciting to me. And it paints a picture of a business that is continuing to expand, which in the long run should create even more value for shareholders. It's clear that, even though the macroeconomic environment has been weak according to management, the business is doing well. And during this time, the firm is even confident enough to return large amounts of capital to shareholders.

The final quarter on its own saw management return $40 million to shareholders in the form of share buybacks. The company purchased 1 million shares during that time, bringing the total number and value of share buybacks in the 2025 fiscal year to 1.7 million and $69 million, respectively. You can expect additional buybacks as time goes on. This is because management announced, on October 10th, a new $200 million share buyback program.

As you will see shortly, the stock is incredibly pricey, so I actually wish that the firm would focus that capital on growth opportunities instead. But the fact that it's in a good position to return such a large amount of capital is, in and of itself, a positive indicator.

2026 is looking bright

2026 is looking to be a fantastic year for the company, even though it faces multiple areas of weakness. Management cited tariff-related uncertainty and broader economic concerns as potentially problematic. In fact, those have already impacted demand in Latin America. The firm also suffered from weaker wind and general construction activity in the Americas. But this has been offset in large part by robust demand for infrastructure, petrochemical, and power generation end markets. In the Asia Pacific region, there was strength as well, though the firm did see a bit of a decline when it came to its EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) regions because of a broader economic slowdown in both central and southern Europe.

Despite these challenges, management expects revenue this year to be between $635 million and $655 million. The midpoint there would translate to a 4.6% increase year over year. On the bottom line, EBITDA is expected to grow from $153.6 million in 2025 to between $158 million and $168 million this year. Operating cash flow, meanwhile, should come in somewhere between $115 million and $120 million, while adjusted earnings per share have been forecasted at between $1.85 and $2. The midpoint there would mean adjusted net profits of about $103.8 million.

Applying these estimates and also applying the historical results that the company achieved last year, we get the chart above. These are multiples that put the company between the fair value range and overvalued range, as I typically see it. To make matters worse, the stock is actually quite expensive compared to other similar firms. The table below shows how the business stacks up against five such enterprises. On a price-to-earnings basis, only one of these was cheaper than it is. But when it comes to the other two trading multiples, four of the five are cheaper than they are at the moment.

Company Price / Earnings Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Enerpac Tool Group 23.4 20.7 15.2
Albany International Corp. (AIN) 28.3 10.9 9.9
Hillman Solutions (HLMN) 87.4 12.2 11.4
Kennametal (KMT) 18.6 8.3 7.6
Standex International (SXI) 50.7 40.6 21.3
Tennant Company (TNC) 24.8 17.1 9.8

Takeaway

By all counts, Enerpac Tool Group is doing a fantastic job. This growth engine is continuing to expand, even though there are challenges like weaker organic revenue, Terra-formulated problems, and more. But this does not translate, at least in my book, to a reason to buy it. The price that you pay should be appropriate for the value that you receive. And that is not what I'm seeing here.

I wouldn't say that I expect Enerpac Tool Group to underperform the broader market moving forward. But I do think we are getting closer to the point where further upside might be challenged by valuation. For this reason, I believe that maintaining it as a Hold candidate is the appropriate choice, though I am not afraid to downgrade the company at some point if need be.

data.title: Sana Biotechnology: A Big Winner In Waiting? Maybe, But It Won't Happen Overnight
data.description:
Investment Overview - Background to Yesterday's Breakout Gains

At the time my last note on Sana Biotechnology, Inc. (NASDAQ:SANA) was published at the beginning of August, its shares were in free-fall, dropping from >$5 to <$3 on news of a fresh fundraising by the company.

Sana raised ~$75m via the issuance of 20.9m shares priced at $3.35 and 1.5m pre-funded warrants. Its share price had only just bounced back from a low of <$1.5, having spiked to ~$4.3 in January, after the company reported that it had successfully transplanted UP421, its allogeneic primary islet cell therapy engineered with its proprietary hypoimmune ("HIP") technology, into a 42-year-old patient living with type 1 diabetes ("T1D") for over three decades, without the use of any immunosuppression.

I remained bullish on Sana, however, despite some reservations - for example, I noted that:

Sana has an accumulated deficit of $1.65bn, and investors may be wondering if all the cash burn has been worth it - while the T1D success should be celebrated, if it is not scalable then there can be no commercial market.

I also noted, however, that Sana has a strong management team with plenty of experience, and concluded that:

Because I am excited to see what can happen with HIP technology, and allogeneic cell therapies - a breakthrough always feels close - I am upholding my Buy rating despite shares now being down 30% since my last Buy call.

I don't see any meaningful catalysts in the near-future to be aware of, so I'd consider holding off buying for the time being, as shares could easily sink back <$2, in my view, but if Sana can secure an IND for SC451 and move it into the clinic, we might start to see some longer-term upward momentum.

It seems I was wrong about the near-term catalysts, even if the catalyst that sent Sana stock soaring to >$6 per share yesterday - up >100% since my August Buy call - may have come from an unexpected source:

Sana Technology a "100-Bagger" - Renowned Hedge Fund Manager Believes So, But This Is A Long-Term Project

Yesterday, Seeking Alpha reported that Canadian hedge Fund manager Eric Jackson said he is long on Sana and that he believed the company could be a "potential 100-bagger," i.e., its valuation could grow 100x higher.

Given Sana's market cap was ~$1bn at the time (it is now $1.52bn at the time of writing), Jackson expects Sana to one day be a $100bn market cap company - an ambitious target, by any standards, but every company has to start somewhere, and I broadly agree that a functional cure for type 1 diabetes, provided it were proven to be safe, would have massive market potential.

Jackson tweeted:

Why the upside is enormous - there are 9m people with Type 1 diabetes globally. If Sana's therapy costs $100k per patient (typical for cell therapy) and reached 10% of them, that's $90bn in revenue potential over time.

The only part I disagree with is the cost - I'd speculate the average cell therapy costs ~$250k, so perhaps >$200bn of revenue potential over time would be more accurate?

Of course, that doesn't mean I am banking on Sana being a "100 bagger." One patient successfully injected and able to produce insulin naturally again is an incredible achievement, but only a small first step on a long, complex, expensive, and risky journey.

Nevertheless, Jackson also tackled some other points that are worth sharing. However, before we consider them, it is worth sharing some quotes from Sana's President, CEO, and Director, Steve Harr, made during a fireside chat in early September at the Morgan Stanley Healthcare Conference. Firstly, a quote also shared by Jackson on the chances of successfully treating diabetics with a gene therapy:

So we have a really rare opportunity to move forward with what looks like a transformational curative therapy. So our goal with this is very simple. A single treatment that leads to euglycemia or normal blood sugars, with no more insulin, no more monitoring, no more immunosuppression for life. And I think we can comfortably say now this will happen. And so what – again, we may not do it. We can make a mistake, or we have safety issues or time, but it will happen.

The CEO discusses how in the past, to cure diabetics (whose disease is effectively caused by a missing pancreatic beta cell, these being the only cells in our body that are able to make insulin):

people started to transplant pancreatic islets from cadaver. So think about an islet as the pancreatic beta cell plus some support cells around it, right? So they could isolate islets and transplant those into people with type 1 diabetes.

And what you saw was, you're now seeing people out 15, 20 years off insulin and doing quite well. But it's not a really scalable or replicable supply source. And you also have the challenge that there aren't that many people for whom lifelong immunosuppression is better than lifelong insulin.

Harr also notes that several groups have successfully created pancreatic islet cells from stem cells using genetic engineering, including Sana itself:

So what is our goal is we've made a gene-modified pluripotent stem cell, and we will grow that into islets. And so the most challenging part of that for us has been making that first cell that's your drug product forever, the master cell bank. We've now accomplished that.

But the CEO stresses that:

-the next problem is how to create trillions upon trillions of such cells, without their being mutated in the cell division process.

And so we spent a lot of time, energy and dollars figuring out how to make these cells and grow them through many, many, many new divisions without seeing problematic mutations emerge.

So that has been the most important safety aspect that's taken us a long time and a lot of time and energy to overcome.

According to Jackson, Sana has:

already created a master stem cell bank that can produce trillions of identical cells with no mutations or tumors after 15 months in animals.

Sana's CEO also seems to believe it is now able to create a master cell bank of cells that can be grown into, e.g., pancreatic islet cells safely with no "problematic mutations," stating that it is:

something that likely gives us a multiple year of competitive advantage and something that should derisk the company dramatically for a long period of time.

Still, several problems remain – how to introduce cells into a patient without the patients' immune system attacking them, causing a potentially fatal condition such as graft vs host disease ("GvHD"); how to administer the therapy in vivo; how to avoid the necessity for immunosuppression (a painful regime that weakens the immune system prior to cells being introduced to prevent GvHD); and, of course, how to fund the R&D necessary to advance the therapy into a pivotal stage study and fund an approval submission?

In terms of introducing cells to patients, the CEO highlights some of the obstacles as follows:

historically, most islet transplants, what happens is they are injected into the portal vein of a patient and then the cells are shot up into the liver. And the challenges with that is, first of all, it has to be done under interventional radiology. So for a disease with 15 million people, it's not that scalable.

The second is that when they go into the liver, they tend to cause clots. And because of that, patients are anticoagulated. So about 5% of patients actually end up in the hospital either from a clot or a bleed. And again, those are things we don't want. We're trying to really do something that is scalable.

So we put the cells in the arm. So we do gene modified stem cells -- sorry, gene-modified islets from a pancreas of a recently deceased person and put them into the muscle in the arm of a person with type 1 diabetes.

Harr confirms, however, that while the single patient Sana has treated in this manner is now "making his own insulin for the first time since 1987," that solution of administration is not scalable. Sana is targeting the muscle, the CEO suggested at the Wells Fargo healthcare conference on September 5th, while admitting that:

exactly how we do that and in what muscle or muscles might want to do muscles is something we're still working through

Looking Ahead - The Obstacles Sana Must Overcome Start With Funding

While many of Jackson's points regarding Sana are valid, in my view, the idea that a solution to curing type 1 diabetes is done and dusted and just requires the time and funding to navigate the regulatory approval process is well wide of the mark.

Sana says it knows what cells it needs to make and how to make them, and it believes it can make trillions of such cells (the average dose is apparently ~1bn engineered cells), and it knows where to introduce the cells into a patient in such a way that immunosuppression may not be necessary, but it cannot be certain that the method of administration can be completely safe, and it has yet to find a scalable solution to administration.

Sana says it hopes to file its investigational new drug ("IND") application with the FDA, requesting permission to try its therapy SC451 – the mass-produced version of UP421 that was successfully administered to a single patient -in an in-human study "as early as 2026."

Sana wants to start with healthier patients – as Harr puts it:

over time, I want every person who has type 1 diabetes to get this drug... It's a very unsatiated patient population. And so early on, we'll start with adults, right? They will quickly move into adolescence, and it will take longer to get into young children. Early on, we will probably again, not have people who've had a heart attack and things like that.

This is all well and good, but funding remains a significant challenge - Harr cites the example of Gilead Sciences (GILD) and its hepatitis C franchise:

You look at the simplest and best example of a scaled cure would probably be Gilead's hepatitis C drug. And if you guys -- if you were -- and that was one where through a very short bolus of time, they were able to treat many, many people with a very, very grievous disease. And society really struggled with it, right? And it wasn't -- in the grand scheme of things, it wasn't that expensive at the end of the day, right?

I take this to be an inference that a drug that cures diseases is not necessarily going to be an all-time best-seller, because it is a one-and-done therapy that may rapidly run out of patients to treat, i.e., become a victim of its own success. Why invest in a drug that may only make sales for a decade or so?

That is a horribly cynical viewpoint, but the CEO's more general point is that Sana needs investors who don't mind funding a "moonshot" opportunity that may provide no short-term returns, as cash is rapidly burned through and only incremental progress is made.

Sana's fusogen platform is attempting to make in vivo administration possible without the need for immunosuppression, and it is a "holy grail" target for many gene therapy companies, for example, CRISPR Therapeutics (CRSP), Intellia Therapeutics (NTLA), Beam Therapeutics (BEAM), and Verve Therapeutics – now part of Eli Lilly (LLY).

If we consider Sana's pipeline above (slide taken from a recent investor presentation), we can see that Fusogen technology is being used in numerous programs besides diabetes, including autoimmune diseases and heme cancers, and there is persuasive evidence that such therapies may work and be marketable.

Nevertheless, in my view, before we talk about Sana being a "100 bagger," we should consider the amount of funding the company may need to get to where it wants to be. The company's accumulated deficit is presently $1.75bn), and my guess is that figure may need to reach $10bn before a major breakthrough opening up a viable commercial opportunity is achievable.

My point is that it may take anything from $10bn to $50bn of investment before Sana begins to represent a bona fide "100 bagger" - the company cannot get there of its own accord without overwhelming amounts of financial support.

Hence, while I celebrate Jackson's comments and the investment in Sana stock they seem to have attracted, I don't share his optimism that Sana stock may be "going to the moon" any time soon. As mentioned, there is a path to success being traced that is theoretically and scientifically credible, but to scale Everest, you need patience and expensive equipment. To "go to the moon" is an order of magnitude more expensive than that.

I hope Sana does get more investment because the CEO's dedication impresses me, as do the accomplishments made by the team to date, but to switch from the single-patient UP421 therapy to the commercially more viable SC451 one will not happen overnight.

It is going to take a lot more tweets from a lot more credible people to keep Sana's share price and its business afloat, but it is just possible that one day, the payoff will be as Jackson describes it. In May 2019, Nvidia stock was worth <$3.5 per share - today it is worth $181, up >5,000%.

data.title: VAW: Materials Dashboard For October
data.description:
This monthly article series provides a top-down analysis of the materials sector based on metrics focusing on value, quality, and momentum. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as Materials Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLB) and Vanguard Materials ETF (NYSEARCA:VAW), whose holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each industry: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for packaging in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in packaging companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.

data.title: Old National Bancorp: Earnings Likely To Plateau After Bremer Acquisition, Maintain Buy Rating
data.description:
Earnings of Old National Bancorp (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:ONB) will receive a big boost from the acquisition of Bremer Financial this year. Subsequent to the merger, the loan balance and the net interest margin will likely plateau. Hence, the recent merger is the only earnings catalyst I can foresee for the next year and a half. Old National Bancorp’s stock price has rallied because of the acquisition. The question arises: Is there still some room left for price upside?

Loan Balance Size to Stabilize After Big Jump in 2Q

Old National Bancorp closed the merger with Bremer Financial Corporation in May 2025, as per a press release. The acquisition, along with organic loan growth of 3.7% annualized, led to loan growth of 31% (126% annualized) till the end of June from the end of March 2025, according to details given in the earnings presentation and the 10-Q filing.

Old National has historically relied on M&A transactions for growth, so I’m not hopeful for loan growth in the short term before the next merger. Over the last ten years, Old National Bancorp has made six M&A transactions, as detailed below. Looking at the spacing between the acquisitions, I think another M&A transaction is unlikely before mid 2026.

Unless there is another acquisition in the latter part of next year, loan growth will likely remain subdued over the remainder of 2025 and 2026, partly because management gives preference to acquisitions and focuses less on organic growth. Moreover, the bank’s market is also currently not conducive to loan growth. Old National Bancorp operates in a number of states in the Midwest and Southeast regions of the United States. Further, the company focuses on commercial and commercial real estate loans.

I think the loan demand will be hurt by tariffs and their effect on the economy. The IMF has raised its USA GDP forecast in its latest World Economic Outlook report to 2.0% in 2025 from 1.8% in April, according to news reports. However, the latest forecast is still below the forecast of 2.2% it gave in October last year. All these forecasts for 2025 are well below the actual GDP growth of 2.8% for 2024; hence, we are set for a significant economic slowdown for this year.

Further, for the district of the Fed of St. Louis, the Fed noted in its Beige Book for October that, “Agriculture conditions are strained and have further deteriorated. The outlook remains slightly pessimistic.” This district spans all of Arkansas and parts of Illinois, Indiana, Kentucky, Mississippi, Missouri, and Tennessee, which are markets for Old National.

Overall, I’m expecting loan growth to be subdued now following the big jump in the second quarter. The second quarter’s jump will be the major net interest income driver through the end of 2026.

Margin to Also Plateau After Recent Hike

Old National Bancorp’s net interest margin jumped up by a hefty 26 basis points in the second quarter of 2025 after declining for most of the last two years. The second quarter’s surge was mostly attributable to the acquisition of Bremer Financial. Hence, the acquisition benefit for Old National’s net interest income is two-fold. Firstly, the loan balance received a boost, and secondly, the net interest margin received a raise.

Much like the loan balance, I’m expecting the net interest margin to be mostly stable from here on out. I’m expecting another 25 basis points of Fed funds rate cuts this year, following the 25 basis points cut in September. Further, I’m expecting 50 basis points of rate cuts in 2026. These cuts are unlikely to have much of an impact on the margin because of the way the rate-sensitivity of assets matches the rate-sensitivity of liabilities.

The loan book is quite rate sensitive. Around 59% of loans are variable or floating-rate, of which 93% reprice within three months, according to details given in the earnings presentation. Additionally, around $7.0 billion of fixed-rate loans and securities are expected to reprice over the next twelve months, representing 11% of total earning assets.

Similar to the loan portfolio, the deposit book is also quite rate sensitive. Checking, NOW, Savings, and Money Market deposits altogether make up around 60% of total deposits. The rates on these types of accounts can be changed regularly. Additionally, around $7.0 billion of time deposits are scheduled to mature or reprice in the second half of 2025, as mentioned in the 10-Q filing. These maturing time deposits represent 13% of total deposits.

As a result of this mostly neutral rate-risk position, I’m assuming the net interest margin will remain stable after jumping up in the second quarter to 3.53%.

Merger to Drive up Earnings

Earnings of Old National Bancorp will receive a big boost from the acquisition of Bremer Financial because it has raised loan balances and the net interest margin. In the third quarter, like the second quarter, earnings can be low because of merger-related expenses. However, once these merger-related expenses have been incurred, earnings will likely surge.

To forecast the non-interest expenses, I’m taking management’s guidance. Management is expecting one-time merger-related expenses of around $194 million, as given in the earnings presentation. Further, management is estimating cost savings of around 30% of Bremer’s 2025E non-interest expenses, as mentioned in the merger presentation. Management expects to realize 25% of these savings in 2025 and 100% in 2026 and thereafter.

To arrive at my EPS estimate for 2025 and 2026, I’m assuming the provision expense will return to normal after the merger-related spike in the second quarter. I’m expecting the provision-expense-to-loan ratio to be around 0.30% annually, which is the same as the ratio for 2024.

Further, I’m assuming the non-interest income will stabilize near the second quarter’s level.

Overall, I’m estimating an EPS of $2.15 for 2025, up by 28% year-over-year. For 2026, I’m estimating an EPS of $2.77, up by 29% year-over-year.

Old National Bancorp is scheduled to announce its third-quarter results on October 22, as per a press release. I’m estimating the EPS to be around $0.60 for the quarter.

Old National Bancorp’s earnings are exposed to the same risks as other banks. The government shutdown can delay key economic data, leading to a postponement of Fed funds rate cuts. This can hurt the US economy and raise provision expenses for ONB. The threatened tariffs on China are another big driver of uncertainty, which can worsen inflation in the US and affect the interest rate trajectory.

Adopting a Buy Rating

I adopted a buy rating on Old National Bancorp back in October 2024. Since then, I haven’t gotten an opportunity to cover Old National again until now. Since my last report, the stock price has risen by more than 17%, mostly because of the Bremer Financial transaction. To find out if there is still some upside potential, I’m using the historical average price-to-earnings (“P/E”) and price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) multiples to value Old National.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple by my EPS forecast gives a target price of $24.60 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple by my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $25.30 for the end of 2025.

The average price target comes out to $24.90, which suggests a 17.0% upside from the October 15 closing market price of $21.31.

Old National Bancorp has paid a quarterly dividend of $0.14 per share since the beginning of 2020. This dividend level suggests a dividend yield of 2.6%. Adding this dividend yield to the price upside gives a total expected return of 19.6%. Hence, a buy rating seems appropriate for Old National Bancorp.

The company’s earnings are set to receive a big boost from the recent acquisition, after which they will likely plateau. My valuation analysis shows that the market has still not fully incorporated the benefits of the merger in Old National Bancorp’s stock price. Hence, I’m maintaining a buy rating on the stock.

data.title: Cardinal Infrastructure Group Pursues IPO To Repay Debt And Expand
data.description:
Cardinal Is Growing From Acquisitions

Cardinal Infrastructure Group Inc. (CDNL) has filed to raise $100 million in an IPO of its Class A common stock, according to SEC S-1 registration information.

CDNL provides a range of wet utility infrastructure services in the state of North Carolina.

The company is growing revenue and operating profit, partially through recent acquisitions.

I’ll provide an update when we learn more about the IPO from management.

What Does Cardinal Do?

Raleigh, North Carolina-based Cardinal Infrastructure Group Inc. was founded to serve homebuilders and general contractors with its full-service offerings of grading, site clearing and wet utility installation services.

Management is led by founder and CEO Mr. Jeremy Spivey, who has been with the firm since its inception in 2013 and has over 30 years of civil construction experience across various aspects of land development and civil project work.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • Wet utility installations.

  • Water, Sewer and Stormwater Systems.

  • Grading and Site Clearing.

  • Erosion Control.

  • Drilling and Blasting.

  • Asphalt Paving and other related site services.

Cardinal has booked fair market value investment of $78.6 million in notes payable as of June 30, 2025, from investors, including Ross Berner and Mark McKinney.

What Is Cardinal’s Market?

According to 2025 figures here and here from the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, the combined US markets for water supply, wastewater and sewage construction had an estimated $86.5 billion annual run rate.

If North Carolina's percentage share of total wet utility construction is 5% due to its higher new housing permits and construction activity, that results in an estimated market size of around $4.3 billion.

The primary factors driving this expected growth are much higher-than-average homebuilding activity in North Carolina, which grew its housing stock by an annual rate of 1.9% from July 1, 2023, to July 1, 2024. (Source: Office of State Budget and Management, North Carolina).

As a result, the demand for wet utility infrastructure has grown to connect the new housing to necessary utility services.

Additionally, the US is estimated to have a need for repair, rehabilitation, and replacement work of wet utility systems exceeding $1 trillion over the next ten years, according to various estimates, with the EPA estimating that water infrastructure alone will need at least $1.2 trillion over ten years (PDF).

Major competitive vendors or industry players include the following firms:

  • AECOM (ACM).

  • Kiewit Corporation.

  • Morgan Corp.

  • Quanta Services, Inc. (PWR).

  • Others.

The company markets its services to homebuilders and general contractors as part of a bidding process for wet utility construction contracts in the state of North Carolina.

The firm's footprint is in the greater Charlotte, Raleigh and Greensboro areas, 'strategically aligned with the North Carolina Research Triangle, which houses numerous educational institutions and knowledge-sector companies that are among the core drivers of job growth in the state.'

So, while Cardinal is a highly focused service provider, other industry players such as Quanta and Kiewit have a larger scale that can yield cost efficiencies and greater leverage in capital markets.

Below is a thumbnail SWOT analysis for Cardinal:

Strengths

  • Vertically integrated for better cost control, strong market presence in North Carolina, and established relationships with major homebuilders.

Weaknesses

  • Geographic concentration in North Carolina, recent expansion through acquisitions, and a capital-intensive business model.

Opportunities

  • Expansion into new geographic markets, increasing vertical integration to concrete and pipe production, industry consolidation, and positive regional demographic growth.

Threats

  • Interest rate changes, macroeconomic effects on housing production, and rising input costs result in contract profitability risks.

Cardinal’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Growing topline revenue.

  • Increasing gross profit and gross margin.

  • Higher operating profit.

  • Variable cash flow from operations.
