As of June 30, 2025, Cardinal had $19.2 million in cash and equivalents and $155.7 million in total liabilities.

The company has generated free cash flow for the twelve months ended June 30, 2025, of $18.1 million.

Cardinal’s IPO Plan

CDNL has filed to sell at least $100 million worth of shares of Class A common stock in an IPO, not including the sale of customary underwriter options.

No existing or possibly new shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring additional shares at the IPO price, at least as of the initial prospectus.

Class A and Class B stockholders will receive one vote per share, although Class B shareholders, the Continuing Equity Holders (operating LLC members) will not have any economic or liquidation rights.

Company, shareholder and executive lockups will be for the typical 180-day period following the effective date of the prospectus.

However, beginning 90 days after the effective date of the prospectus, Rule 144 and Rule 701 shareholders may be entitled to sell up to 1% of the greater of the outstanding shares of the firm’s Class A common stock or the average weekly trading volume of its Class A common stock reported during the four calendar weeks preceding the filing of notice of sale.

According to Cardinal’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

to purchase newly issued LLC Units

to repay [an as yet undisclosed amount of] borrowings outstanding under our New Credit Facility

the remainder, if any, for general corporate purposes, which may include funding for acquisitions, working capital requirements, capital expenditures, and the repayment, refinancing, redemption, or repurchase of indebtedness or other securities.

(Source: SEC).

The firm’s presentation of the company roadshow is not yet available.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, management said there are currently no legal claims that would have a material adverse impact on its financial condition or results of operations.

Listed underwriters of the IPO are Stifel, William Blair and D.A. Davidson & Co.

Cardinal Is Growing From Recent Acquisitions

CDNL seeks US public capital market investment to pay down debt and fund its growth plans in North Carolina’s active homebuilding markets.

The company’s financials have produced increasing topline revenue, growing gross profit and gross margin, increased operating profit and fluctuating cash flow from operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended June 30, 2025, was $18.1 million.

General & Administrative expenses as a percentage of total revenue have fluctuated within a range and its General & Administrative efficiency multiple rose a bit to 6.7x in the most recent reporting period.

The firm currently plans to retain future earnings to fund management's growth plans and the company's working capital requirements.

CDNL’s recent capital spending history shows it has spent materially on capital expenditures as a percentage of its operating cash flow.

The firm’s growth strategy includes expanding into new markets, adding complementary services in product manufacturing, and continuing to selectively acquire small to mid-sized firms.

To that end, CDNL’s order backlog has grown substantially, from $401 million at the end of 2023 to $512 million at the end of 2024 and $643 million as of June 30, 2025.

However, some of that backlog growth is due to its recent acquisition activities rather than just organic growth.

The market opportunity for providing wet utility infrastructure services in North Carolina is significant and has been growing at a faster rate than most other US markets due to pro-growth housing policies.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its ‘emerging growth company’ status, which enables management to disclose less information to shareholders.

Many of these types of companies have performed in a mediocre way post-IPO.

I’ll provide a final opinion when we get management’s pricing and valuation assumptions.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Oracle: A Valuation That Relies On Many Pieces Falling In The Right Place
data.description:
Downside Thesis

Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) has announced that it expects almost half a trillion cloud orders during its Q1 2026 earnings call. Consequently, the stock market has richly rewarded Oracle with a nearly $250 billion additional market value, from $688 billion to a peak of around $934 billion market value, an almost 35% increase. The Wall Street Journal would later reveal that a significant portion of Oracle's booked orders will come from OpenAI. While this may seem like a Nvidia-like (NVDA) blowout earnings for Oracle, the significant reliance on one customer and the clear excess that the AI market, which is slowly becoming a bubble in my opinion, is demonstrating a significant risk to its valuation. Therefore, I will call a 'Sell' rating for Oracle.

The Importance Of Understanding the Capital Cycle Principle When It Comes To AI

While I don't doubt AI's prospects to generate more wealth for the world due to its potential to increase employees' productivity, therefore, increase the standard of living for all, I believe that, as with any disruptive innovation, the stock market tends to be very optimistic in its prospects, which often results in excessive capital being invested in an industry. The danger of this excessive insertion of capital into any industry, regardless of its age, new or mature, is that it often leads to overcapacity, which can affect the industry's profitability. Due to less-than-anticipated demand to absorb the capacity meant for it, industry participants have no choice but to cut prices, creating a price war in the industry.

Now, why does this capital excess repeat itself despite the countless lessons that the history of market excesses has taught us? I believe certain natural forces repeatedly make these kinds of mistakes. After all, we are dealing with human nature that seems to repeat this cycle of optimism to pessimism like a pendulum going left and right and vice versa. From the excess of the tulip mania, excessive speculation before the Great Depression, the dot-com bubble, and the financial crisis. Now, what drives these market excesses? It comes from the market's thirst for short-term gains, the investment bankers' vested interest to keep the boom going, disregarding its consequences, and the pro-cyclical behavior of the industry participants, who need to find a way to redeploy the excessive capital their investors are giving them through sky-high valuations.

I believe that this is already at full display in the AI market with securitization datacenters, vendor round-trip-like equity financing, the sky-high valuation of AI energy providers with zero revenues, and the Midas touch of the AI market leaders. The principles of game theory are also exacerbating this excess in the AI market, further fueling additional capacity buildouts despite the uncertainty in its ROI.

The problem with this pro-cyclical behavior is that it drives overcapacity into an industry, resulting in supply outstripping demand. This is when the share price starts to underperform due to sky-high market expectations. It is only after rationality has returned and excess capital has exited the industry through consolidation and the retirement of excess capacity that the remaining companies can generate profit above their cost of capital. The stock price often performs well after these periods of pessimism. I believe AI is currently at the most optimistic part of the cycle. Even some industry observers and participants are starting to worry about its bubble-like status. Nonetheless, the worst part of the capital cycle can be avoided if we ask a simple question: 'Is there a demand that will absorb the increasing capacity?'

Big Reliance On OpenAI To Deliver

After Oracle's earnings call, the Wall Street Journal would later reveal that around 2/3 of Oracle's almost $500 billion backlog belongs to OpenAI. This presents a significant customer concentration risk for Oracle, especially for a startup that has yet to turn a profit.

The deal commits OpenAI to a rent-an-infrastructure arrangement with Oracle Cloud Infrastructure or OCI. This is around a $60 billion per year expense for OpenAI or a total of $300 billion on a five-year agreement, which will start in 2027.

How will OpenAI pay for this deal and even sustain it? Through equity financing, which is reminiscent of the dot-com bubble, venture capital, and its future revenue growth.

OpenAI projects its revenue will grow at a 5-year CAGR of around 68%, from $13 billion this year to around $174 billion in 2030.

I am always wary of a management's eternal optimism in regards to their business prospects. You will barely find any CEO who is not optimistic about their business. But, this doesn't suggest that optimism is not warranted. Optimism is needed. Imagine the mood of the employees if the CEO doesn't believe in the company's prospects. Optimism is a required trait of any successful company. But it is the sky-high optimism that I'm always wary about, rather than the realistic one, as there are economic forces that often hinder this kind of optimistic future projection from materializing. This is perfectly explained by Benjamin Graham in his book Security Analysis.

It must be remembered that the automatic or normal economic forces militate against the indefinite continuance of a given trend. Competition, regulation, the law of diminishing returns, etc., are powerful foes to unlimited expansion, and in smaller degree opposite elements may operate to check a continued decline.

I do not suggest that OpenAI can't achieve this future revenue projection with certainty. However, an investor only needs to look through the lens of an outside view to realize how hard it is for OpenAI to achieve this.

Technology companies, especially software-oriented ones, often have an easier time or route in achieving the $100 billion sales milestone. This ability can be attributed to the fact that technology companies can rapidly scale their business at a low marginal cost. Good examples are Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), Google (GOOG), and Meta (META), which took 43 years, 21 years, 19 years, and 17 years, respectively, to reach the $100 billion sales milestone. Clearly, there is a clear trend that the time it took for newer technology firms to reach the $100 billion sales mark is getting shorter and shorter. Thus, there is clear empirical evidence that OpenAI could achieve its revenue projection. However, there are additional factors that enable these companies to achieve the $100 billion sales milestone in such an ever-shorter time period. Each of these companies represents a disruptive innovation that opened up an uncontested market for these companies to dominate. Microsoft in operating systems, Amazon in e-commerce, Google in search engines, and Meta in social media.

Another significant factor in the success of these companies is the lack of an entrenched and profitable competitor during their ascension to market dominance. All these companies have competed against similarly sized companies backed by venture capital firms during their early years. Compare this to the current predicament of OpenAI, which faces well-capitalized big tech companies like Microsoft, Google, Meta, and Amazon, along with other niche AI startups such as Anthropic in Enterprise and Perplexity in Search. Despite the first-mover advantage of OpenAI, it will compete with more formidable competitors that have profitable core businesses that AI complements well with. This means that AI could be a loss leader for these companies, as it makes their core businesses better, unlike OpenAI, which solely relies on the AI to generate revenue.

The Real AI Demand That Remains Elusive

Let's say that OpenAI's first-mover advantage enables it to gain a significant competitive advantage against these strong competitors. There is one crucial factor that I believe will hinder it from meeting its revenue projections, and this goes back to the question posed earlier, but only in a different form: 'Is there a demand that will absorb this cost?' Or, in OpenAI's case, 'Is there a demand that will absorb its $300 billion commitment to Oracle?'

Often, I read or hear from analysts or industry participants that AI demand is off the charts, immense, or through the roof. If we are talking from the perspective of hyperscalers or AI model builders, demand is strong. However, the real demand that will absorb the AI capacity being built today lies further beyond the hyperscalers and AI model builders. And that is the Enterprises and Consumers. These two customers are where OpenAI currently generates its revenue from, with the majority coming from the consumers. There are active efforts to diversify OpenAI's revenue streams, such as in advertisements and agentic AI.

If there is ever hope for OpenAI to achieve its revenue projection, enterprise AI adoption is a must. However, there are still significant hurdles that need to be overcome before AI adoption among enterprises comes to fruition. Currently, 95% of enterprise AI pilot runs still fail, 97% still struggle to generate ROI, and there is still minimal evidence that AI is delivering the productivity gains it meant to deliver to justify enterprise adoption. This could change in the future as the technology matures and industry standardization comes into play. However, there is no assurance of how long this will happen, and for now, there is a clear hurdle that needs to be overcome before this materializes.

The usual problem with new technology is that it often takes longer than expected for its market adoption. This problem is exacerbated when the industry involved is very dynamic or rapidly changing. The investment in technology to enable this innovation may become obsolete sooner than expected due to the rapid iteration of better ones. This famously happened in the dot-com bubble. The continuing release of better technology, methods, and techniques allowed later iterations to accommodate more network traffic, further worsening the capacity glut caused by the older buildouts. Eventually, the anticipated demand would come into fruition, enabling the surviving enterprise from the dot-com bubble to prosper.

A Massive Revenue Gap

According to Bain Capital, there is currently a $800 billion revenue gap that needs to be filled to profitably fund the current datacenter build-outs.

This is a broader market scope, but OpenAI's market leadership exposes it to this problem. Thus, let us dissect each assumption.

Just looking at the first column, I see a flaw in this assumption of a 'Move All On-Premise IT to the Cloud.' I don't ever see this happening. Factors such as regulation, latency, performance, and data privacy often prevent enterprises from moving fully to the cloud despite the supposed cost savings over an on-premise implementation. Let's move over to the other column, which is a 20% reduction from the cost of sales, marketing, customer support, and R&D. As mentioned earlier, as with any new technology or innovation, adoption can often take longer than expected. These kinds of assumptions to justify huge amounts of capital spending, especially in an uncertain new industry like AI, are like a ticking time bomb. It implodes whenever the assumptions don't end up materializing, resulting in excessive depreciation costs and write-offs, and, much worse, if funded by debt. Nonetheless, let's assume that the 20% cost savings do end up materializing. There is still an almost $1 trillion revenue gap to fill, which I believe is quite a stretch.

Increasingly Risky Balance Sheet

For the year 2026, Oracle has guided for almost $35 billion in CAPEX to support its backlog of almost $500 billion. This CAPEX spending constitutes an almost 60% of Oracle's revenue in 2025. S&P Global Ratings projects that Oracle's CAPEX will reach more than $60 billion in 2027 and peak in 2028.

For the year 2025, Oracle generated an EBITDA of around $24 billion. Cash from the OpenAI deal won't start coming in until 2027. Clearly, there is a gap between Oracle's cash generation and the CAPEX spending required to support its backlogs. To fund these backlogs, Oracle will most likely incur more debt, which can make its already aggressive balance sheet riskier when compared to other hyperscalers unless the company issues equity, which makes sense due to its sky-high valuation, in my opinion.

Oracle's core business possesses inherent stability due to the high switching cost involved in moving enterprise data and databases to other vendors. This stability enabled Oracle to carry more debt on its balance sheet. Under normal operating conditions, I do believe that this balance sheet is adequate and optimized, as Oracle's strong cash flow generation provides a cushion from the risk associated with debt. Additionally, in strong companies, an investor should generally prefer more debt than equity, as the former is cheaper and makes the latter more productive or efficient. I believe a good yardstick of a 3x to 5x Debt/EBITDA ratio is manageable for strong companies. However, during times of rapid industry change, such as in AI, an investor should prefer a more conservative balance sheet, like those of Microsoft and Google. The inherent uncertainty associated with newer technology or an industry with no clear demand yet makes debt very risky for any company in a turbulent industry.

Valuation

Oracle is the most expensive stock relative to significant AI hyperscalers like Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta. This is evident in the chart below.

I use EV/EBIT to account for the difference in capital structure between these companies. Understandably, Oracle's considerable premium over market leaders like Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta can be due to its backlog of almost $500 billion. However, as articulated throughout this article, I believe that this valuation possesses a significant risk from its heavy reliance on OpenAI.

Moreover, at Oracle's current valuation, which is almost 2x that of Meta, which I believe is a beneficiary of AI rather than disrupted by it, like that of Google, the significant premium is a representation of the high market expectations. I believe an investor should be very wary of companies whose valuation is embedded with high expectations, especially on growth rates. Growth rates tend to revert sooner than the market expects. I believe that there are just a lot of pieces that need to go into the right places to justify this valuation. Therefore, I will call a 'Sell' rating for Oracle. The reason that it's not a 'Strong Sell' is that Oracle still possesses a strong and stable core business. I believe Oracle's strong core business can provide a foundation and cushion for the company if OpenAI ends up not meeting its obligations.

Risk To The Downside Thesis

A significant risk to the downside thesis is if OpenAI does end up meeting its projection, thereby meeting its commitment with Oracle. After all, we cannot ignore the fact that OpenAI has a first-mover advantage in AI and is the clear market leader. This advantage could further translate into future success for OpenAI and Oracle.

Also, despite the clear capital excesses that the AI market is demonstrating, a significant portion of capital being employed in the market is still composed of equity. Currently, most hyperscalers still have a healthy balance sheet and strong profitability to support their AI capital spending. In contrast to the dot-com bubble, which the current AI market is always compared to, a significant portion of capital employed is funded by debt, which previously resulted in severe implosions after the bubble popped.

data.title: Applied Digital: The Overlooked Powerhouse Behind The AI Boom
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Applied Digital Corporation (NASDAQ:APLD) is at the center of one of the largest capital shifts in history, the move from energy-based mining to compute-based AI. Though still widely categorized among crypto-associated infrastructure names, the company itself went through a structural transformation some time ago. It's now an AI infrastructure utility, constructing, owning, and leasing high-density hyperscale-worthy GPU data centers.

What distinguishes Applied Digital from others is obvious and deep as it capitalises on power, not price volatility. In a world in which demand for AI exceeds available power, that virtue becomes its moat. Each megawatt leased becomes in effect a 15-year annuity, backed by hyperscalers that are prepared to pay top dollar for velocity, cooling, and grid stability.

From Power to Rent: The Strategic Pivot

Over the last two years, the company's direction indicates a careful move from dependency on blockchain toward specialization in AI. Leadership knew the next capacity bottleneck wouldn't be compute shortages but power density, how to support GPUs at scale with enough cooling and reliability. In its proprietary campus architecture, Polaris Forge, the thesis in Applied's proprietary campus architecture, lives up to the notion.

Its initial location in Ellendale/ North Dakota, Polaris Forge 1, spans 400 MW of core IT load in a long-term agreement with CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV), which translates into about $11 billion in committed lease income. CoreWeave's expansion option, which was exercised earlier this year, confirmed Applied's design and execution leadership, specifically in cooling efficiency and construction speed. Every phase of the campus is designed specifically for ultra-dense racks above 50 kW per cabinet, with closed-loop liquid cooling boasting an industry-leading PUE of 1.18 and almost negligible water usage.

It's not a proof of concept. The initial 100 MW building is in advanced stages of completion, with lease recognition set for initiation at the year-end 2025. The second and third buildings, each of 150 MW size, will be constructed subsequently in mid-2026 and early 2027. By the third building's initiation, the NOI run rate from one campus at steady state approaches $500 million per year.

Concurrently, Polaris Forge 2, a 300 MW site near Fargo scalable to 1 GW, broke ground. Initial capitalization originates through Macquarie Equipment Capital, and lease talks are well underway with an investment-grade hyperscaler in an anchor tenancy for the entire campus. The company's current active pipeline standing surpasses 4 GW, highlighting demand certainty and the growing build capacity at Applied.

These projects put Applied at the forefront of three interactable trends: hyperscaler AI CapEx that's now over $350 billion per year, perpetual U.S. grid-wide shortages of power, and the geographic shift in the concentration of the world's largest data centers from high-cost coastal hubs to low-cost high-availability locations such as the Dakotas.

The Quarter of Inflection: From Construction to Cash Flow

Q1 fiscal year 2026 was the point of operational inflection. Revenue increased 84% year-over-year, and $64.2 million was mainly due to $26 million in tenant fit-out revenue, the installation preparatory phase in advance of lease onset. Those dollars are low in the margin, but they are the indicator of hard-won progress in the contract execution area. The other $38 million came from the company’s traditional hosting business, which operates at full potential in its 286 MW of blockchain-enabled data centers.

Figures also reveal a balance sheet in redefinition. Property and equipment thus add up to $1.6 billion in assets deployed, physical assets that are ready for accretion in revenues. Despite a net loss of $27.8 million (-$0.11 per share), the adjusted net loss and the adjusted EBITDA were $7.6 million and $0.5 million, respectively, and break-even in an indicative readout of leverage in operations with very high building activity.

The quiet catalyst in the capital structure. Eliminating serial equity raises, the $5 billion preferred equity facility from Macquarie Asset Management (MAM) fully capitalises Polaris Forge 1 and provides a scalable template that can be used for future sites. Accompanied by project financing at 70–80% loan-to-cost and blended SOFR + 400–450 bps rates, the structure can take in $20–$25 billion in total build capacity. Regular capital recycling at the subsidiary level in the pattern of the better REITs de-risks the capital cycle of Applied.

This configuration reconfigures the way in which Applied should be considered. Rather than valuing it on short-term EPS, the correct lens is future NOI and net lease cash flow. With contracted revenue tied up for 15 years and a pipeline of power-secured campuses, the economics are repositioning the company's economics into a utility-like yield, stable, inflation-indexed, and anchored with hyperscaler credit quality.

Valuation Mirage: Expensive Now, Cheap Later

At around $38 per share and a $9.8 billion market value, APLD seems stretched on near-term multiples with a 33x multiple forward sales and a forward EV/EBITDA multiple of 160, versus sector medians of 3.5x and 15.6x.

Analysts' estimates of FY2026 $298 million revenue and FY2027 $543 million revenue miss the recurring revenue from the leases of CoreWeave alone, which implies over $700 million yearly revenue at maturity. Add a second hyperscaler tenant at Polaris Forge 2 and the cumulative run-rate NOI can approach $800–$900 million in FY2028. Therefore, with a conservative 20x P/S multiple for APLD and using a conservative $1 billion estimate, three years from now could reach $20 billion in market cap, suggesting a double upside potential in 2-3 years' time.

Thus the current price valuation is the preceding pre-revenue process in a yield company. Investors are purchasing no current earnings, they are purchasing a window into contracted, inflation-indexed cash flow from multi-gigawatt camps.

Risks Beneath the Megawatts

Applied's opportunity is massive, but so are the needs for its implementation. In the pipe at multiple locations are operations of 700 MW with attendant coordinated deliveries of substations, transformers, and cooling modules.

Although management secured most of the supply chain nearly two years in advance, even minor delays in permitting or grid connections could disrupt lease commencement timelines. Tenant concentration remains a near-term threat. CoreWeave represents close to 100% of contracted revenue today. Future hyperscaler leasing in Polaris Forge 2 shall be the catalyst for diversified exposure.

Once secured, it shall leave the revenue base materially derisked and further propel APLD's long-term story adoptions. From a financial standpoint, debt levels of $687 million appear sustainable against asset value, all the more so with the imminent NOI ramp and access to the structured capital of Macquarie. The asset-rich, low-churn model of Applied lends itself well to streamlined refinancing cycles after the commencement of operational cash flows. The broader picture also favors sustainability.

The US Department of Energy estimates 40-50 GW incremental data-center demand in the year 2030, the equivalent of some forty nuclear reactors. Hyperscale CapEx continues growing at a pace greater than the grid's capacity, and low-cost inland locations offered by Applied Digital are the prime supply centers in the AI infrastructure value chain.

Takeaway

Applied Digital is transforming into an AI infrastructure utility with $11 billion in contracted revenue, $5 billion in institutional financing, and 4 GW of pipeline capacity. Its transition from construction to recurring rent positions it as a future REIT-style compounder powering the AI era’s data and energy backbone.

data.title: Fluence Energy: Why I Moved To Hold After This Strong Comeback (Downgrade)
data.description:
Article Thesis

I wrote about Fluence Energy, Inc. (NASDAQ:FLNC), and I initiated a small position at ~$16.9 at the beginning of December last year. However, that seems like it was in another era. I saw their BESS (Battery Energy Storage Systems) as especially useful for energy consumption optimization in a decade with increasing and fluctuating electricity demand from sources like data centers. And this tailwind still plays, combined with other advantages like a short commissioning time:

On the other hand, another tailwind was perceived to be the combination of BESS and solar energy, because it can provide a continuous power supply of energy, which means that solar tax credits will be gradually phased out by the new U.S. administration. And then another headwind hit: high tariffs imposed on China (even perceived as a blockade for a period), with negative effects on their already thin gross margins.

But the abysmal plunge to ~$3.6 in April was an obvious exaggeration, perhaps driven by some shorts. After all, Fluence had a flourishing ex-U.S. business, and even in the U.S., BESS were (and are) still important for the next years, with or without solar.

But now, only a few months later, Fluence is trading above $20, after the announcement that The AES Corporation (AES), one of their major shareholders, will likely be acquired by the deep-pocketed BlackRock, Inc. (BLK).

After reevaluation of developments in recent months and Fair Value, I decided to move FLNC stock to Hold, and I will detail below why.

Business Highlights

As mentioned in the introduction, Fluence has a strong momentum in Asia Pacific and EMEA, with achievements like:

  • Selected for a big Australian battery storage project, powered by its flagship Gridstack system, and including Mosaic (asset performance management software) and Nispera (bidding software).
  • Opened utility-scale energy storage system manufacturing plant in Vietnam, in partnership with ACE Engineering, for their Gridstack Pro and Smartstack Systems.
  • The EU renewables arm of Ukraine's DTEK Group selected Fluence for a battery storage system in Poland.

There is a mixed picture in the U.S. due to tariffs and regulatory headwinds, as mentioned in the introduction. Not only were their margins affected, but there was also a slower-than-expected backlog conversion due to this huge policy volatility, especially concerning China. However, Fluence is advancing with their integrated U.S. supply chain:

And there were some tactical victories lately:

  • All paused U.S. contracts reactivated after tariff/regulatory clarity (but…is there clarity after the recent re-escalation?).
  • Delivered the first fully U.S.-manufactured energy storage system.
  • Partnered with Torch Clean Energy to deliver a solar-plus-storage facility (yes, solar is still active, but for how long?).

Tariffs and OBBBA (One Big Beautiful Bill Act)

Fluence seems to be mostly satisfied by the long-term impact of OBBBA, with limited Chinese competition:

However, the picture is rather mixed:

  • (+) Energy storage is exempt from the phase-out of tax credits, indeed, as pointed out, for example, by JPM analysts.
  • (-) OBBBA’s aim to sunset solar tax credits will also affect BESS to some extent (long-term headwind), and domestic competition is considered higher by Mizuho in the next 1-2 years (short-term headwind).

Despite progress with the U.S. integrated supply chain, high tariffs for China (and huge volatility surrounding them) are a big problem because there are still some battery cells imported from China:

As of the filing date of this Report, covered imports relevant to our energy storage systems will be subject to the 10% China-specific “reciprocal tariff”, plus the current China-specific 20% “fentanyl-related tariff”, plus the 3.4% base tariff on all countries, plus the applicable Section 301 tariff. We have exposure to these Chinese tariffs, as we currently import a number of components, including battery cells from China, into the United States for customers and projects in the United States. We also import other components for our energy storage solutions from other countries, including Vietnam and countries in Europe, that are subject to tariff uncertainty and/or increased tariffs from the U.S. over the course of the past year.

The situation seemed to be at least clear, even if not China-friendly, at one point during this year:

…Only that we are now hearing again about 100%+ tariffs.

Financial Analysis

Q3 revenue was

“~$100M below plan due to a slower ramp at new U.S. module and enclosure facilities, with production ~15% under expectations. Both met July milestones and are on track to reach target capacity by year-end.”

It needs to be noted that ~46% of their pipeline is still coming from the Americas, that is why these Chinese tariffs are very important for their business.

Bet let’s go a bit further, and let’s see what I consider to be the biggest problem raised by these tariffs, a razor-thin gross margin:

When gross margin is under 15%, of course, any shock can have huge ripple effects. And it’s not only tariffs, but lithium prices (LC:COM) are also very volatile and can have an impact.

Q3 adjusted gross margin (~0.5 bps above GAAP gross margin) was 15.4%, reflecting solid execution particularly in Europe and Asia, regions with higher gross margin. However, looking at the FY’25 guidance, we see a different picture:

We see again a low expected gross margin for FY’25 due to higher U.S. revenues:

On the margins for the fourth quarter, the fourth quarter has a lot of U.S. revenue. A lot of the revenue in the fourth quarter comes from the U.S., and it has been affected by the new tariff. It's roughly -- we disclosed in the last call, roughly $30 million, which generally represents 1%. So that's what's driving, I will say, the softer margin number for the year.

For 2026, they have a backlog of ~$2.5 billion, and they will provide formal guidance in November, but gross margin is seen at the same depressed levels (10%-12%).

However, they see a restoration to 10%-15% again from H2 ’26, based on new cost structures:

I think new contracts come in, they will reflect the new cost structure, and we should see them going back to the normal margin levels of 10% to 15% that we have set for ourselves. So, it should not -- this is a temporary situation. There might be some in the first quarter of '26 that still have these issues. So there will be some lingering ones, but I think the majority will be done by between this quarter and the first quarter of '26.

Finally, an important look at their operating expenses, since their gross margin is so low. OpEx growth is estimated long-term at half of revenue growth. However, for 2026 it will be relatively flat YoY because their costs reflect the expected growth for 2025 that they were unable to deliver.

Fair Value

To see the huge ripple effects that any shock can have for a business with such thin gross margins, I built a DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) model for two scenarios: a slightly optimistic scenario and a pessimistic one.

First, some common assumptions. I will model the consensus revenue estimates for the next 5 years:

A CAGR of ~20% seems plausible, with some 2025 backlog delayed for 2026, with a flourishing business in EMEA and Asia Pacific, but with some pressures in the US. Then, I model slightly declining revenue growth, from 16% in 2030 to 3% in 2034.

Other model variables:

  • OpEx growth as in their model: flat for 2026, then half of revenue growth.
  • Growth in perpetuity: 2.5%, close to GDP growth, but not exactly, for a no-moat company.
  • WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital): 10.4%, derived from a 12.5% cost of equity. I generally start from 11%-11.25% for a no-moat hardware company (their software business is minor), but I add ~0.5 bps for each of the following factors: huge policy uncertainty, razor-thin gross margins, and untrustworthy guidance in the past.
  • Tax rate: 21%.

Then, I vary only the gross margin for these two scenarios: for the slightly optimistic scenario I take the higher end of their guidance, and for the pessimistic one the lower end.

Why and How I Am Out

Strategy #1: In my stocks portfolio (100% stocks, no options, for a better comparison against the S&P 500), I initiated a small position (meaning ~0.25x) at ~$16.9, as mentioned in my initial article, which I closed yesterday at ~$20.5, as mentioned through a comment. ROI was ~21.3% in ~10 months, while the S&P 500 advanced ~10% during this period.

The reason is that the stock was very close to my slightly optimistic Fair Value ($22.2) and ~20% above a weighted average Fair Value. While I still hold positions in moaty stocks like Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) or Teradyne, Inc. (TER) even after they passed above my Fair Value, Fluence is nowhere close.

Strategy #2: In my options portfolio, I had another even smaller position, as described in my initial article, a combination of 30 shares and an options strategy:

  • Long FLNC 13 Put Jan’26: debit $210.
  • Short FLNC 16 Put May’25: credit $218.

Per total, $8 credit for the two options.

With Fluence plunging towards $13 (the lower strike price) in February, I had to close this position for a significant loss of ~$180, because the short 16 Put entered ITM (In-The-Money) and the long 13 Put was able to offset only a small part of that.

It’s generally better to close these spreads when the price is heading towards the lower strike price (towards $13 in this case), the loss would have been higher at the first expiration in May (and maximum possible loss with Fluence at $0 would have been $300, the distance between the two spreads, with both Puts without extrinsic value).

I usually don’t have losses from these spreads, and even if sometimes they are at a loss in a first iteration like in this case, I just roll them down for a still consistent overall profit. I presented this rolling strategy in articles about Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(GOOGL) or Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ), for example. But again, Fluence is nowhere close, I had about enough of this huge policy volatility, and I just kept the corresponding 30 shares, which I have closed today at $21.38 (pre-market), for ~134 profit.

Per total: 134+8-180 = ~$38 loss, or about -7.5% from this second strategy for the same period (only that for a very small position).

Takeaway

Fluence Energy has a strong momentum in EMEA and Asia-Pacific, but the picture in the U.S. is mixed, with short-term headwinds from U.S.-China tariffs affecting their razor-thin gross margins and long-term headwinds from the phase-out of solar tax credits.

Revenue is expected to grow at ~20% CAGR for the next 3-4 years, with some 2025 backlog delayed for 2026. Gross margins are expected to remain depressed through 2026, with recovery to 10-15% possible in late 2026 as new cost structures take effect.

In a slightly optimistic scenario, with an adjusted gross margin of 12% next year, then 15% until 2034, my Fair Value estimate is $22.2. In a pessimistic scenario, with an adjusted gross margin of 10% next year, then 10% until 2034, I have a Fair Value estimate of $6.25. An average Fair Value should probably be tilted a bit towards the slightly optimistic one, because I operate with a rather pessimistic cost of equity (12.5%) for both scenarios.

I closed my Fluence positions with the stock about 20% above a weighted average Fair Value. I do hold stock positions above my average Fair Value estimate, but only for moaty stocks with more predictable growth ahead, and the same is valid for rolling over options strategies.
